name: Hypothermia / Osborn (J) Waves
identifyBy: Prominent J (Osborn) waves — positive deflection at J point, 'camel hump' appearance
J wave amplitude proportional to degree of hypothermia
Osborn waves most prominent in V4–V6 and inferior leads
Prolonged PR, QRS, and QT intervals (hypothermia slows all conduction)
Shivering artefact: muscle tremor baseline wander may be present
Risk of VF when core temp <30°C
management: Passive rewarming for mild hypothermia (>32°C): blankets, warm environment, remove wet clothing
Active external rewarming for moderate (28–32°C): warm blankets, forced warm air, warm IV fluids
Active internal/core rewarming for severe (<28°C or VF): warm humidified O2, warmed IV saline, bladder/gastric irrigation, ECMO in refractory cases
VF in hypothermia: CPR + defibrillation; if no response, continue CPR until rewarm to >30°C ('not dead until warm and dead')
Avoid rough handling — hypothermic myocardium is highly irritable
Target core temperature monitoring (oesophageal or rectal)
pearls: Osborn wave amplitude inversely correlates with core temperature — bigger J wave = colder patient
Hypothermia can mimic death: no pupil response, asystole — do not pronounce until fully rewarmed
'Not dead until warm and dead' — core temp goal >30°C before ceasing resuscitation
Atrial fibrillation is common in moderate hypothermia — rate often spontaneously resolves with rewarming
Shivering artefact may mimic VF on the monitor — check patient pulse before defibrillating
differentials: Early repolarisation (J point elevation but NO bradycardia, normal intervals)
Brugada pattern (V1–V2 only, coved ST, not temperature dependent)
Hyperacute STEMI (convex ST, no J wave, normal intervals)
Benign Osborn-like waves in hypercalcaemia or normal variant athletes
